title: Cron Expression Parser
description: Parse any cron expression into plain English and see the next 10 execution times. Supports standard 5-field cron syntax.

howToUse.label: Enter a cron expression
howToUse.description: Type a 5-field cron expression: minute (0–59), hour (0–23), day-of-month (1–31), month (1–12), day-of-week (0–7).

howToUse.label: Use wildcards and operators
howToUse.description: Use * for "any", */n for "every n", ranges (1–5), and lists (1,3,5) in any field.

howToUse.label: Check the schedule
howToUse.description: The tool shows a human-readable description and the next 10 scheduled run times based on the current time.

faq.question: What is cron syntax?
faq.answer: Cron is a time-based job scheduler in Unix. A cron expression has 5 fields: minute (0–59), hour (0–23), day-of-month (1–31), month (1–12), and day-of-week (0–7, where 0 and 7 are Sunday). Each field can use *, numbers, ranges (1-5), lists (1,3,5), or steps (*/5).

faq.question: What does */ mean?
faq.answer: */n means "every n units". For example, */15 in the minute field means "every 15 minutes", and */2 in the hour field means "every 2 hours".

faq.question: What are common cron expressions?
faq.answer: * * * * * = every minute | 0 * * * * = every hour | 0 0 * * * = daily at midnight | 0 9 * * 1 = every Monday at 9am | 0 0 1 * * = first day of every month

faq.question: How are day-of-month and day-of-week combined?
faq.answer: When both day-of-month and day-of-week are non-wildcard, the job runs when either condition is met (OR logic). When only one is non-wildcard, it restricts the schedule.
